NodeTestRules.keywords: node-test, runner, assertions, ai_first_tests, contract_testing, unit_testing, mocks, snapshots, bdd_mapping, case_flow, isolation
NodeTestRules.Mission:
Canonical rules for writing tests on the built-in `node:test` runner (`node --test`) for every execution-agent in the project.
Goal: produce a contract-focused, isolated, deterministic, semantically annotated test suite that another AI agent can read, trust, run, and extend without reverse-engineering.
Baseline testing rules layer; referenced from task tickets generated by `task-scaffolding` for verification scope = node:test. Domain-specific testing conventions (UI, e2e Playwright, integration with infra) NOT covered here; they live as separate directives layered on top.
**A test is executable learning context.** Names, structure, anchors, and local comments must reveal WHAT is under protection, WHAT must not regress, and WHERE the scenario is fragile. Tests are CODE — they obey the same machine-first annotation language used in production code (see `coding/typescript-rules`). The repo reads as one system, not two dialects.

Belief_State.Axiom: Test the public behavior promised by the contract, NOT the private mechanics of the implementation. A refactor that preserves behavior must not break the test suite. Tests bound to internals become an obligation for every refactor and lose the ability to catch regressions of external behavior.

Isolate via mocks/stubs of external dependencies + assert behavior, externally visible effects, and interaction contracts. Do NOT reach into protected/private internals of the SUT. Mock-based isolation guarantees determinism; asserting on the interaction contract keeps test focus on the boundary the real contract crosses.

For emitter / subscription APIs observe emitted event objects at the public boundary; assert the contract surface (`type`, payload envelope, `detail`, ordering when promised). Do NOT inspect listener arrays, internal emitter state, or registration mechanics. Internal listener structures are an implementation detail; the contract is what the emitter promised to expose.

For cancellation APIs assert the observable contract of the public signal or returned handle (`aborted`, `reason`, downstream reaction). Do NOT inspect controller wiring, linked listeners, or private cleanup state. Cancellation is a contract about what happens downstream; wiring mechanics may change without changing the contract.

A test file is executable learning context for the next agent. Names, structure, anchors, and local comments must reveal which behavior is under protection, what must not regress, and where the scenario is fragile. A test suite that reads like documentation saves context for the next agent and makes regressions visible immediately.

Tests are code. Structural anchors and local intent comments in tests MUST stay compatible with the machine-first annotation language of production code. The repo reads as one system, not two unrelated dialects.

`Arrange-Act-Assert` is useful but not a law. Canonical pattern is a repeatable `Case Flow`: `SETUP → TRIGGER → OBSERVE → ASSERT`, with only the phases that add clarity. AAA is the common short form, not a hard law. Rigid AAA forces empty phases or splits obvious scenarios — that is noise, not signal.

For non-trivial tests TRIGGER and ASSERT are mandatory. SETUP, OBSERVE, CLEANUP appear only when they add clarity. TRIGGER is the point where the contract boundary is crossed; ASSERT is the only place the contract is verified. Without them a test proves nothing.

**Mandatory: for every non-trivial case explicitly decide which phases are present, then wrap EACH present meaningful phase in paired AI anchors `START_[CASE]_[PHASE]_[INTENT]` / `END_[CASE]_[PHASE]_[INTENT]`.**
Do NOT silently collapse SETUP / TRIGGER / OBSERVE / ASSERT when a phase carries its own setup policy, observation logic, or diagnostic boundary. Equally, do NOT ritualize anchors around a single obvious statement with no extra intent.
Phase anchors are the machine grammar for test refactoring: they let an agent cut and replace phases by pairs without an AST.

Case-level phase anchors do NOT replace normal structural anchors inside the phase body. Test logic with non-trivial branching, loops, retries, or local fallback behavior → apply the same intentful inner anchor style as production code (see `coding/typescript-rules` `AX_ANCHOR_COVERAGE_NONTRIVIAL`). Phase anchors cover case flow; inner anchors cover local policy. Two levels of the same system.

OBSERVE is the preferred place to build an aggregated `actual` object when a single rich diff beats several isolated assertions. A clear place for aggregation makes the diff structure of the test predictable and stops observations from being scattered across the case.
Do NOT force a fake AAA split or empty phase blocks when the scenario is already obvious in compact form. An empty phase is noise that erodes mandatory anchors on truly non-trivial cases.

**Mandatory: every non-trivial `it` block opens with a learning brief** before executable statements. 1–3 short lines from the machine-first payload.
Allowed payload: `purpose`, `consumer`, `invariant`, `side effect`, `failure mode`, `non-goal`, plus test-specific `contract` and `observation focus`.
**Non-trivial case** = async with setup; retries/fallbacks/errors; emitted events / external side effects; more than one meaningful observation; nested control flow that another agent could misread at a glance.
Brief may be omitted ONLY when title + visible phase structure + direct assertion already expose the protected behavior without hidden policy.
Title describes behavior; brief explains why this contract is under protection and which edges matter.

Skip the brief for trivial scalar tests where title, direct input, and a single assertion already make the scenario self-evident. A brief on a trivial case is noise that weakens the signal of briefs on hard cases.

Choose the assertion shape that produces the most useful failure report. Prefer the simplest direct assertion on the real contract surface before inventing aggregated wrappers. A failure report is the first debug-signal channel.

Aggregating several related observations into one object is valid ONLY when it simplifies the test and improves the diff. If the returned result already exposes the right contract shape — assert it directly and keep orthogonal observations separate. Synthetic objects for the sake of synthetic objects hide the business point of a test.

Default protocol: first ask whether a direct assertion on the real contract surface gives the clearest failure. Introduce an OBSERVE phase or aggregated object only when it clearly improves readability, preserves the contract shape, or yields a better diff. Aggregation is a tool used on demand.

For event-driven scenarios assert the emitted event object or collected event sequence directly. Build synthetic summaries only when they improve the diagnostic diff without hiding the contract surface. The event sequence IS the contract.

Pick the simplest direct match by shape: - `assert.strictEqual()` — scalars. - `assert.deepStrictEqual()` — structured results. - `assert.throws()` / `assert.rejects()` — thrown failures. - `t.assert.snapshot()` / `t.assert.fileSnapshot()` — ONLY for large stable serializable outputs (generated text, HTML, JSON documents, codegen).
Each assert function has a unique diagnostic signature. A mismatched choice (e.g., snapshot instead of strictEqual for a scalar) degrades the failure report.
Use `assert.match()` instead of `includes()` for partial error / string checks. `assert.match()` shows the real value in the failure output; `includes()` boolean-folds the result and loses context.

If the SUT returns `Result`, use `unwrap()` or `unwrapSync()` on success paths to keep the assertion surface compact and contract-focused. `unwrap` collapses «check success + extract value» into one call, leaving the assert solely on the business shape.

Error cases may use a small focused set of assertions, especially when error text or `cause` is partly dynamic. A strict full-equal on a dynamic error produces flaky failures with no diagnostic signal; focused matches keep the test stable and informative.

Do NOT fragment one coherent contract into many tiny assertions if one structured comparison would show the whole drift. Symmetrically: do NOT force aggregation when it makes the test harder to read. Choose by diagnostic clarity.

All external dependencies passed into the SUT MUST be mocked or stubbed. Public API only — do NOT call protected/private methods, do NOT read private state. Without mocks isolation and determinism break; reaching into private state violates `AX_CONTRACT_OVER_IMPLEMENTATION`.

Prefer `mock.fn()` (native node:test API) to track calls and emitted behavior. Native API requires no extra dependencies and is integrated with the runner (cleanup, assertion helpers), reducing risk of runtime drift.

For HTTP-heavy tests: - Use `setupMockAgent()` from `utils/test/mock-http.ts`; call `cleanup()` in `afterEach`. - When `setupMockAgent()` is used, keep request-specific intercepts INSIDE the case `SETUP`. - If HTTP mocks are configured manually and the SUT may use `axios`, set `axios.defaults.adapter = 'fetch'` in shared setup.
Reading fixture files is allowed. Writing files inside unit tests is NOT. Writes from a test make the test runtime stateful, break parallelism, and leave artifacts in FS that mutate between runs.

A mock is allowed when the dependency is external (network, FS, process, external system, time, randomness) or an explicit injection point of the SUT. For PURE in-process modules within the same library/layer — prefer the REAL module to avoid masking real integration bugs. A mock is a tool to isolate from the external world, not from a neighboring module. Mocking a neighbor freezes an assumption about its behavior that may silently drift.

Coverage stance: measure CONTRACT coverage, not line coverage. Mandatory minimum per public method/function: 1. At least one happy-path scenario. 2. At least one boundary scenario (boundary value, edge of input domain) when the contract has boundary semantics. 3. At least one failure-path scenario per declared `@throws` / rejected promise.
Ignored: thin pass-through wrappers without hidden policy, type-only constructs, dead branches excluded by `AX_MINIMAL_ERROR_SURFACE` in coding-rules.
Type-check or coverage cannot run because of an environment / project blocker → declare the blocker EXPLICITLY; do not simulate success. A silent skip turns the completion gate into fiction.

File naming: `[subject].test.ts`. Default shape: `describe(subject)` → optional shared hooks → nested `describe(method)` → `it(case)`. A `Test Graph` (header comment with a map of cases) is RECOMMENDED for multi-method or non-trivial files; OPTIONAL for tiny focused ones.

Where to put what: - Repeated SUT construction / shared environment setup → `beforeEach`. - Scenario-specific mocks, inputs, mutable fixtures → case `SETUP` phase. - Teardown that must run after every case → `afterEach`.
Use `beforeEach`/`afterEach` ONLY when they remove repeated setup/teardown. If a dependency or mock is case-specific, keep it inside the case `SETUP`.
Anchor names describe intent, not raw syntax. `START_FETCH_ITEM_OBSERVE_RETRY_SUMMARY` — yes; `START_FETCH_ITEM_ASSERT_2` — no. Uppercase, high-signal tokens.
Phase contains non-trivial nested logic → add inner intentful anchors there too. Phase anchors cover case flow; inner anchors cover local policy. Same two-level patch grammar as production code.
Do NOT add anchors to trivial one-line cases that remain obvious without them. An anchor around a single line is noise.

When an anchored test block protects a non-obvious invariant, retry policy, fixture constraint, or failure boundary → place the intent comment IMMEDIATELY after the opening `// #region START_...`, just like in production code.
One test covers one scenario. Avoid duplicate cases that prove the same behavior. Duplicates create false confidence in coverage and double the maintenance cost when the contract changes.

Never write step-by-step human narration like `Step 1`, `Step 2`, `Step 3` inside a case. Comments only where they increase recoverable intent for the next agent; do not duplicate the test title, assertion text, or obvious setup facts. Phase anchors already make the steps machine-visible.
A test focuses on observable behavior, not implementation trivia. A test bound to trivia breaks under harmless refactors.
Test names, comments, anchors, and helper variable names — English only. Mixed languages break grep and comprehension.

Finalizing a test = re-checking isolation, naming, anchor pairing, snapshot placement, and the diagnostic quality of failures, then running type-check and coverage. If something cannot run — declare the blocker EXPLICITLY.

Snapshots ONLY for large stable serializable outputs (generated text, HTML, JSON documents, codegen results). Do NOT replace simple scalar or small object equality with a snapshot. A snapshot hides the contract under a «diff blob»; for small equalities it degrades diagnostics.

Keep snapshot files in a dedicated `snapshots/` directory next to the test area. Update snapshots ONLY through the runner's snapshot-update flow; do NOT edit them manually. Manual edits enable silent contract drift.

Definitions.Definition: A case is non-trivial (requires opening brief + phase anchors) if any of: async behavior with setup; retries / fallbacks / errors; emitted events / external side effects; more than one meaningful observation; nested control flow that another agent could misread at a glance.

Phase anchor naming: `// #region START_[CASE]_[PHASE]_[INTENT]` and a paired `// #endregion END_[CASE]_[PHASE]_[INTENT]`. PHASE ∈ {`SETUP`, `TRIGGER`, `OBSERVE`, `ASSERT`, `CLEANUP`}. CASE — uppercase short scenario identifier; INTENT — uppercase intent token.

Closed vocabulary for intent comments inside tests (same base as production code, plus test-specific): `purpose`, `consumer`, `invariant`, `side effect`, `failure mode`, `non-goal`, `contract`, `observation focus`.

NodeTestRules.BDD_Mapping_Hint:
Public contract between `task-scaffolding` and any execution-agent writing tests under this directive.
- **`BDD_CANONICAL_CASE_NAME_BINDING`** — canonical case name from ticket MUST be used verbatim as the `it(...)` title. Execution-agent changes a name → MUST update `Test Scenario Coverage` in the ticket BEFORE closing. Leaving canonical names that don't exist in real test file is forbidden (per `AX_TICKET_HAS_BDD_AND_TESTS` in `task-scaffolding` + `AX_BDD_NAME_DISCIPLINE` in `task-execution`). - **`BDD_SCENARIO_TO_IT`** — one BDD scenario → one `it(...)` case (1:1). Don't merge several BDD scenarios into one case (breaks traceability). Don't split one scenario into several cases without explicit derivation. - **`BDD_FEATURE_TO_DESCRIBE`** — `Feature: [Component Behavior]` → outer `describe(...)` (or semantically equivalent SUT name). Each `Scenario:` becomes a separate `it(...)`. - **`BDD_GIVEN_WHEN_THEN_TO_CASE_FLOW`** — `Given` → SETUP phase + (optionally) `beforeEach` if shared. `When` → TRIGGER phase. `Then` → ASSERT phase. `And` after `Then` → additional assertion in same ASSERT OR separate OBSERVE phase if aggregation needed. Phase-level anchors mandatory for non-trivial case. - **`BDD_ERROR_SCENARIO_BINDING`** — error/rejection scenario → `assert.throws()` / `assert.rejects()` in ASSERT phase. Assert error message via `assert.match()` (per `AX_PARTIAL_STRING_VIA_MATCH`). If scenario refers to a domain error class — assert both `instanceof` AND a message pattern. - **`BDD_VERIFICATION_SURFACE_GUARDRAIL`** — `Test Scenario Coverage` carries a `Runtime Fidelity` marker. Tests under this directive cover ONLY `contract-only` and `simulation-backed`. `runtime-hook-required` and `e2e-required` outside scope; record deferred coverage owner EXPLICITLY in Execution Log. - **`BDD_DEFERRED_OWNERSHIP_HONESTY`** — BDD scenario cannot be materialized as a `node:test` case (e.g., requires e2e harness) → silent skipping forbidden; add deferred-ownership reference in `Test Scenario Coverage`.

Pattern.Intent: Trivial scalar case without an opening brief: title + direct input + a single assertion already expose the scenario. Phase anchors are kept by mandatory protocol.
Pattern.Why: Title + direct input + single strictEqual make the scenario self-evident → opening brief skipped per `AX_IT_TRIVIAL_SKIP_BRIEF`. Phase anchors preserve machine grammar even on trivial case.

Pattern.Intent: Async scenario with retry policy: opening brief records invariant + failure mode; phase anchors separate setup mocks, trigger, and assert; orthogonal observation (attempt count) kept separate from main contract assertion.
Pattern.Why: Brief reveals retry invariant and failure mode; main contract assertion is direct (`deepStrictEqual` on result); orthogonal observation (`attemptCount`) sits next to it without diluting the contract shape.

Pattern.Intent: Error-path scenario: focused assertions via `assert.rejects()` + `assert.match()` on the error message; no `try/catch + flag` antipatterns.
Pattern.Why: Error case uses focused set: `instanceof` + `match` on stable part of message; dynamic correlation id NOT asserted. TRIGGER + ASSERT merged because `assert.rejects` both triggers and asserts; forced split would yield empty TRIGGER (per `AX_NO_FAKE_AAA_SPLIT`).

Pattern.Intent: Native `mock.fn()` for tracking calls + assertions on interaction contract without inspecting SUT's internal state.
Pattern.Why: Demonstrates `AX_PREFER_NATIVE_MOCK_FN`, `AX_EVENT_CONTRACT_BOUNDARY` (assert on event payload via public listener), and `AX_DEFAULT_DIRECT_ASSERTION_PROTOCOL` (orthogonal observations as separate assertions, not synthetic merge).

Pattern.Intent: Scenario with a rich result where OBSERVE phase normalizes result + side-effect log into one diagnostic slice. Fixture is read from FS (allowed); writing files forbidden.
Pattern.Why: Aggregation in OBSERVE justified: single `deepStrictEqual` shows drift in both text and milestones. Reading a fixture allowed; writing is not.

Anti_Pattern.Bad: Test inspects `client._inflightCount` (protected/internal field) using `@ts-expect-error` to bypass encapsulation.
Anti_Pattern.Why_Bad: Violates `AX_CONTRACT_OVER_IMPLEMENTATION`, `AX_ISOLATION_WITH_BEHAVIORAL_EVIDENCE`. A refactor renaming `_inflightCount` to `_pending` breaks the test without changing the contract.
Anti_Pattern.Good: Subscribe to `client.on('reservation.requested', e => events.push(e))` and assert on `events.length` + `events[0].idempotencyKey`. Public lifecycle event is the observable; internal counter is not.

Anti_Pattern.Bad: `let threw = false; try { await client.reserveStock(''); } catch (e) { threw = true; assert.ok((e as Error).message.includes('idempotency')); } assert.ok(threw);`.
Anti_Pattern.Why_Bad: Manual try/catch + boolean flag instead of `assert.rejects()` (`AX_ASSERT_API_CHOICE`). `includes()` on error message (`AX_PARTIAL_STRING_VIA_MATCH`). Test can silently pass if code stops throwing — `threw` stays false; only fallback gate is `assert.ok(threw)`.
Anti_Pattern.Good: `await assert.rejects(() => client.reserveStock(''), (error) => { assert.match((error as Error).message, /idempotency.*empty|empty.*idempotency/i); return true; });`.

Anti_Pattern.Bad: `const cart = new Cart()` shared between tests; test names in Russian («добавляет товар»); `Шаг 1: добавляем` / `Шаг 2: проверяем` comments; second test depends on state from first.
Anti_Pattern.Why_Bad: Shared mutable state breaks isolation (`AX_ISOLATION_WITH_BEHAVIORAL_EVIDENCE`); creates order dependency. Non-English names/comments (`AX_ENGLISH_ONLY_NAMES_AND_COMMENTS`). Step-by-step human narration (`AX_TESTS_NO_HUMAN_NARRATION`). No `beforeEach`, so order dependency is structural.
Anti_Pattern.Good: `let cart: Cart; beforeEach(() => { cart = new Cart(); });` then independent tests with English titles like `should expose count = 1 after a single add` and direct assertions.

Anti_Pattern.Why_Bad: Snapshot for a scalar (`42`) — violates `AX_SNAPSHOT_USAGE_GATE`. Failure shows diff of snapshot blobs instead of «42 ≠ 41». Manual review of a snapshot for every scalar change is unnecessary ceremony.
Anti_Pattern.Good: `assert.strictEqual(result, 42)` — direct, clear, no snapshot file overhead.

Workflow_Outline.Step: Read contract (Port/Adapter/function) + ticket's `Test Scenario Coverage`. For every public behavior derive happy-path, boundary, failure scenarios. BDD scenarios in ticket are normative source of case names.
Create test file (`[subject].test.ts`), imports, shared hooks, `describe` groups. Add Test Graph comment for multi-method or non-trivial files.
For each `it`, decide trivial vs non-trivial. Non-trivial → opening brief, emit relevant Case Flow phases, anchor each present phase.
Start with simplest direct check. Several related outputs/effects belong to one contract surface → consider OBSERVE phase + aggregated diff. Otherwise keep orthogonal observations separate.
Re-check isolation, naming, anchor pairing, snapshot placement, diagnostic quality of failures. Run type-check + tests with coverage. Environment blocker → declared EXPLICITLY.

Hook.Purpose: Run entire test suite via native node:test runner.
Hook.Expected: Exit 0; all cases pass; no TODO/.skip without explicit deferred ownership in the ticket.

Hook.Purpose: Run a single test file during iterative development.
Coverage report to verify contract coverage per `AX_COVERAGE_BY_CONTRACT_NOT_BY_LINE`.
Hook.Expected: Exit 0; report shows public methods with ≥1 happy + ≥1 boundary (when applicable) + ≥1 failure-path. Coverage cannot run → declare blocker EXPLICITLY.

Hook.Purpose: Watch mode for iterative test-driven work.
Hook.Expected: Watcher active; re-run fires on every change to a test file or module under test.

Hook.Purpose: Type-check the whole project BEFORE running tests (prevents runtime SyntaxError under native TS).
Smoke-grep for forbidden patterns: human narration `Step N`, `includes(` on error message, manual try-catch flag.

NodeTestRules.Reward_Criteria:
✅ Every non-trivial `it` opens with a learning brief from allowed payload (purpose / contract / invariant / failure mode / observation focus / side effect / non-goal / consumer). ✅ Phase anchors paired, intent-named, cover every present meaningful phase of non-trivial case. ✅ Trivial scalar cases carry no opening brief; title + direct assert self-evident. ✅ Simplest direct assertion chosen (`strictEqual` / `deepStrictEqual` / `throws` / `rejects`); aggregation only when it improves the diff. ✅ Error cases use `assert.rejects()` + `assert.match()` (no try/catch + flag, no `includes()`). ✅ External deps mocked/stubbed; SUT exercised through public boundary; protected/private state NOT inspected. ✅ Native `mock.fn()` used for interaction tracking; HTTP-heavy tests use `setupMockAgent()` + `cleanup()` in `afterEach`. ✅ Snapshots only for large stable serializable outputs; live in `snapshots/` next to test area; updated through runner flow. ✅ Every BDD scenario from ticket's `Test Scenario Coverage` has 1:1 mapping to `it(...)` case with canonical case name. ✅ BDD `Given/When/Then` maps into Case Flow `SETUP/TRIGGER/ASSERT` (with optional `OBSERVE` for aggregation). ✅ Coverage measured by contract: ≥1 happy + ≥1 boundary (when applicable) + ≥1 failure-path per public method. ✅ English-only names and comments; no `Step N` / human narration; no duplicate cases. ✅ `beforeEach` / `afterEach` actually remove repeated setup; case-specific logic stays in case `SETUP`. ✅ Type-check / coverage cannot run → blocker declared EXPLICITLY rather than masked as success.
❌ Test inspects protected/private/internal state of the SUT (contract-boundary breach). ❌ Manual `try/catch + flag` instead of `assert.rejects()` or `assert.throws()`. ❌ `includes()` on `error.message` instead of `assert.match()`. ❌ Snapshot used for a scalar or small object. ❌ Mocking a neighboring in-process module without justification — hides real integration bugs. ❌ Writing files from a unit test. ❌ Shared mutable state between tests without `beforeEach` reset. ❌ Phase anchors missing on non-trivial case OR used around trivial one-line cases. ❌ Anchor name restating syntax (`START_FETCH_ITEM_ASSERT_2`) instead of intent. ❌ Opening brief on trivial case OR missing brief on non-trivial case without explicit justification via title/direct assert. ❌ BDD scenario from ticket has no corresponding `it` case OR mismatched canonical case name without ticket update. ❌ Tests written for `runtime-hook-required` / `e2e-required` scenarios at `node:test` level without explicit deferred-ownership note. ❌ `console.*` in test (test logs go through project memory-logger or `t.diagnostic()`); human narration; duplicate cases; non-English names/comments. ❌ Type-check / coverage failed silently without EXPLICIT blocker statement.
